title: Statistical Potential for Identical Fermions: Emergent Attraction and Pauli Crystal Formation
authors: Kawon Lee, Sangeun Oh, Young Woo Choi, Jeong-Hyuck Park
abstract: We show that the thermodynamics of $N$ identical fermions maps onto that of distinguishable particles governed by a collective statistical potential -- the microscopic origin of degeneracy pressure. Known to be purely repulsive for ${N=2}$, this potential develops attractive contributions for ${N\geq 3}$. Its minima coincide with Pauli crystal configurations, providing the energetic origin of these structures. For large $N$, the dominant force is attractive on inner shells and repulsive on outer ones -- not of two-body origin. The global minimum undergoes discrete melting transitions at specific temperatures.
summary: This paper shows that the statistics of identical fermions can be mapped to a classical system with an effective 'statistical potential' that includes emergent attraction for N >= 3. This attraction provides the energetic origin for the formation of structured 'Pauli crystals' and drives discrete phase transitions as temperature changes.
main_problem: Investigating whether 'Pauli crystals' in non-interacting fermions have an energetic origin rather than being purely kinematic consequences of antisymmetrization.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that the statistical potential for N >= 3 fermions develops emergent attractive components, providing an energetic basis for Pauli crystal formation and discrete melting transitions.
method: The study uses a classical mapping of N-fermion thermodynamics onto distinguishable particles via a statistical potential, employing semiclassical expansions, numerical optimization (L-BFGS-SB), and analytical derivations using the Mehler kernel.
model_or_system: A system of N identical, non-interacting fermions in a 2D isotropic harmonic trap, analyzed through an effective statistical potential (V_stat) and a total potential (V_total).
key_observables: One-body density, conditional density, pairwise statistical forces (attractive vs. repulsive), structural order parameter (r_min), and the ratio of attractive to repulsive forces.
important_parameters: Particle number (N), temperature/inverse temperature (phi = beta*hbar*omega), and dimensionality (D).
main_assumptions: The mapping is exact for the harmonic oscillator and holds at leading semiclassical order for generic potentials; the system is assumed to be non-interacting fermions.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows geometric conditions for attraction; Figure 2 compares single-particle and conditional densities for N=6; Figure 3 shows the dual attractive/repulsive force structure for N=55; Figure 4 tracks temperature-dependent force evolution and order parameters; Figure 5 presents a phase diagram of force dominance.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of Pauli crystals, defines the statistical potential mapping, provides analytical derivations for the harmonic oscillator, presents numerical results for various N and temperature regimes, and concludes with a phase diagram and physical implications.
why_interesting: It challenges the standard view of degeneracy pressure as purely repulsive and identifies a many-body emergent attraction, which could impact the understanding of structural transitions in trapped quantum gases.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Geometric origin of emergent attraction for N = 3, shown as a D = 2 illustration of a dimension-independent condition. The force on particle 1 from particle 2 changes sign depending on the position of the third particle relative to the antipodal ball of ⃗x1, ⃗x2 (in D = 2, the dashed disk with ⃗x1, ⃗x2 as diameter endpoints). (a) When ⃗x3 lies outside the ball (r2 23 +r2 31 > r2 12), the bracket factor in Eq. (12) is positive and ⃗F2→1 is repulsive, reducing to the two-body result (11) as r13, r23 →∞. (b) When ⃗x3 lies inside the ball (r2 23 + r2 31 < r2 12), the bracket reverses sign and ⃗F2→1 becomes attractive.

FIG. 4. Temperature dependence of pairwise statistical forces and structural order parameter at the minimum of Vtotal(X) for N = 55; forces are in units of ℏω/a0. Four panels: (a) to- tal, attractive, and repulsive force sums; (b) order param- eter rmin = mina |⃗x⋆ a|, the radius of the innermost particle; (c) maximum pairwise force magnitude (colored by type); (d) number of attractive and repulsive pairs. The maximum pairwise force crosses over from repulsive to attractive at low temperature (Table I); rmin exhibits discontinuous jumps sig- naling structural transitions (G→I→P; see SM, Figs. FIG. 3. N = 55 at φ = 2. Top: Minimum of Vtotal(X) with pairwise forces overlaid. Bottom: Dominant force on each particle. Inner-shell particles (30 bonds, red) have attractive dominant force directed toward neighboring shells; outer-shell particles (23 bonds, blue) have repulsive dominant force di- rected outward. Mutual pairs (in which each particle is the other’s strongest partner) are drawn as a single bond, so the total count 30+23 is below N = 55. The strongest pairwise force is attractive.

FIG. 5. Phase diagram of the globally strongest pairwise force in the (N, kBT/ℏω) plane for N = 2, 3, · · · , 55; lower temper- atures lie on the left. Red squares: max |Fatt| > max |Frep|; blue circles: max |Frep| > max |Fatt|. Horizontal dotted lines mark closed-shell particle numbers. Closed shells with N ≤45 are repulsion-dominated at all temperatures; some open-shell configurations and the largest closed shell N = 55 develop at- tractive dominance at low temperature.
